content: The eccentricity of the hyperbola which passes through the points $(3,0)$ and $(3 \sqrt{2}, 2)$ is
explanation:
Let the equation of the hyperbola be $\frac{x^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}-\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1$.

Since this passes through $(3,0)$ and $(3 \sqrt{2}, 2)$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{9}{a^2}=1 \Rightarrow a^2=9 \\ & \text { and } \frac{18}{a^2}-\frac{4}{b^2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{18}{9}-\frac{4}{b^2}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow b^2=4 \\ & e=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^2}{a^2}}=\sqrt{1+\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{\sqrt{13}}{3} \end{array} $$

content: If the tangent at the point $(2 \sec \theta, 3 \tan \theta)$ to the hyperbola $\frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{y^2}{9}=1$ is parallel to $3 x-y+4=0$, then the value of $\theta$ is
explanation:
$$ \frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{y^2}{9}=1 $$

Here, $\mathrm{a}^2=4, \mathrm{~b}^2=9$

Equation of the tangent at $(2 \sec \theta, 3 \tan \theta)$ is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x(2 \sec \theta)}{4}-\frac{y(3 \tan \theta)}{9}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x \sec \theta}{2}-\frac{y \tan \theta}{3}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 x \sec \theta-2 y \tan \theta=6 \end{aligned} $$

Slope of this tangent $=\frac{-3 \sec \theta}{-2 \tan \theta}=\frac{3}{2 \sin \theta}$

Slope of line $3 x-y+4=0$ is 3 .

Since this line is parallel to the tangent, their slopes are equal.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{3}{2 \sin \theta}=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \theta=30^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

content: The foci of a hyperbola coincide with the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{9}=1$. The equation of the hyperbola with eccentricity 2 is
explanation:
Given equation of ellipse is

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{9}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow a=5, b=3 \\ & e=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^2}{a^2}}=\sqrt{1-\frac{9}{25}}=\frac{4}{5} \end{aligned} $$

Foci $\equiv( \pm \mathrm{ae}, 0) \equiv( \pm 4,0)$

For hyperbola, $\mathrm{e}^{\prime}=2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Foci } \equiv( \pm 4,0) \equiv\left( \pm a e^{\prime}, 0\right) \\ & \Rightarrow a e^{\prime}=4 \Rightarrow a=2 \\ & b^2=a^2\left(e^{\prime 2}-1\right)=4(4-1)=12 \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore \quad$ Equation of required hyperbola is

$$ \frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{y^2}{12}=1 $$

content: The foci of a hyperbola coincide with the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{9}=1$. The equation of the hyperbola with eccentricity 2 is
explanation:
$$ y=X \sin (6 t+5)+Y \cos (6 t+5) $$

Differentiating w.r.t. t, we get

$$ \frac{d y}{d t}=6 X \cos (6 t+5)-6 Y \sin (6 t+5) $$

Differentiating w.r.t. t, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-36 \mathrm{X} \sin (6 \mathrm{t}+5)-36 \mathrm{Y} \cos (6 \mathrm{t}+5) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^2 y}{\mathrm{dt}^2}=-36 y \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d}^2 y}{\mathrm{dt}^2}+36 y=0 \end{aligned} $$

content: The X and Y intercepts of the tangent to the hyperbola $\frac{x^2}{20}-\frac{y^2}{5}=1$ which is perpendicular to the line $4 x+3 y=7$, are respectively
explanation:
Given line is, $4 x+3 y=7$

$\Rightarrow$ Slope of line $\left(m_1\right)=\frac{-4}{3}$

$\Rightarrow$ Slope of tangent $\left(\mathrm{m}_2\right)=\frac{-1}{\mathrm{~m}_1}=\frac{3}{4}$

Equation of hyperbola

Given, $\frac{x^2}{20}-\frac{y^2}{5}=1$

$$ \Rightarrow a^2=20, b^2=5 $$

Using tangent equation,

$$ \begin{aligned} y & =m x \pm \sqrt{a^2 m^2-b^2} \\ & =\frac{3}{4} x \pm \sqrt{20\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^2-5} \\ & =\frac{3}{4} x \pm \sqrt{20 \times \frac{9}{16}-5} \\ & =\frac{3}{4} x \pm \sqrt{\frac{45}{4}-5} \\ & =\frac{3 x}{4} \pm \sqrt{\frac{25}{4}} \\ y & =\frac{3 x}{4} \pm \frac{5}{2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) \end{aligned} $$

Put $y=0$, We get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 0=\frac{3}{4} x \pm \frac{5}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mp \frac{5}{2} \times \frac{4}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow x=\mp \frac{10}{3} \end{aligned} $$

$\Rightarrow x$-intercept is $\left(\mp \frac{10}{3}, 0\right)$

Now, put $x=0$ in (i), we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & y=\frac{3}{4}(0) \pm \frac{5}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow y= \pm \frac{5}{2} \end{aligned} $$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}$-intercept is $\left(0, \pm \frac{5}{2}\right)$

$\therefore \quad x$ and $y$ intercept of the tangent are $\left(\mp \frac{10}{3}, \pm \frac{5}{2}\right)$

content: The eccentricity of the hyperbola $25 x^2-9 y^2=225$ is .......
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have equation of hyperbola }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 25 x^2-9 y^2=225 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{x^2}{9}-\frac{y^2}{25}=1 \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

On comparing with $\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$, we get

$$a^2=9, b^2=25$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Eccentricity, } e & =\sqrt{1+\frac{b^2}{a^2}} \\ & =\sqrt{1+\frac{25}{9}} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{34}}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $P\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ is a point on the hyperbola $x^2-y^2=a^2$, then $S P$. S'P $=$ .............
explanation:
Given, equation of hyperbola

$$x^2-y^2=a^2\quad\text{... (i)}$$

If $P\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ is a point on Eq. (i), then

$$x_1^2-y_1^2=a^2\quad\text{... (ii)}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Now, } \quad S P=e x_1-a \\ & \text { and } \quad S P^{\prime}=e x_1+a \\ & \therefore \quad S P \cdot S P^{\prime}=\left(e x_1-a\right)\left(e x_1+a\right) \\ & =e^2 \cdot x_1^2-a^2 \end{aligned}$$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =2 x_1^2-a^2\left(\text { since }, \text { for } x^2-y^2=a^2, \theta=\sqrt{2}\right) \\ & =2 x_1^2-\left(x_1^2-y_1^2\right) \text { [using Eq. (i)] } \\ & =x_1^2+y_1^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the lengths of the transverse axis and the latusrectum of a hyperbola are 6 and $\frac{8}{3}$ respectively, then the equation of the hyperbola is ............
explanation:
We have,

Length of transverse axis $2 a=6 \Rightarrow a=3$

and length of latusrectum, $\frac{2 b^2}{a}=\frac{8}{3}$

$\Rightarrow 2 b^2=\frac{8}{3} \times 3=8 \Rightarrow b^2=4$

$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \text { Required equation of hyperbola is }\\ &\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 \Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{9}-\frac{y^2}{4}=1 \Rightarrow 4 x^2-9 y^2=36 \end{aligned}$
